insight: Arms Race is cost-efficient attack but not fast and temporarily reduces pressure on the opponent. A rule of thumb is to buy at most 3 Arms Race units (reaching ~10 damage), because beyond that, the engineers given to the opponent all get immediate defensive value, negating the benefit.
author: ruinedshadows
context: Discussion about Arms Race strategy and when buying multiple copies becomes counterproductive.

insight: In high-economy sets, player 1 slightly favors because P1 generally has better blue and red lines, and the early conduit advantage for P2 is less relevant at high econ since both players end up floating enough gold to be inefficient anyway.
author: ruinedshadows
context: Discussion about whether high-econ sets favor P1 or P2.

insight: Blood Phage is valuable primarily as a Drone plus a fast Tarsier (5R attacker) even without using its click ability; a fast Tarsier at 5R would be a busted unit on its own. Buying a second Animus purely to power Blood Phage clicks is generally worse than simply building higher economy from the start.
author: shadourow
context: Discussion about the optimal use of Blood Phage and whether its click ability justifies buying a second Animus.

insight: DD DD is a legitimate P2 opening in certain sets: for example, DD DD DBA is a good start in Hellhound sets, and DD DD BBD is used for Drake. It can also be correct to delay green commitment (e.g., in Tatsu sets with DD/DD/DAA) to keep strategic options open.
author: amalloy
context: Discussion of P2 openings and when passing on early conduit is correct.

insight: A Grimbotch-only rush is inherently limited because Grimbotches die out eventually, capping damage at roughly 8 per turn, which a Wall player can weather. Mixing in Tarsiers for sustained attack is necessary for the rush to be viable.
author: amalloy
context: Review of a game where a player attempted a Grimbotch-only rush and lost.

insight: When opening with a red rush, spending red aggressively from turn 1 is critical; opening DD1 (holding a drone instead of spending red immediately) is a poor way to rush Grimbotches, as it wastes the tempo advantage the rush requires.
author: amalloy
context: Game review discussing misplay of holding a drone on turn 1 instead of spending red in a Grimbotch rush.

insight: Cryo Ray charges should be used very liberally: spending 6 Cryo Ray charges to freeze a Centurion and deny its absorb, dealing 3 effective damage, is generally worth it. Only the final remaining charge should be held back.
author: namington
context: Game review where the value of aggressive Cryo Ray usage was discussed.

insight: Clicking a Thermite Core (or similar damage-reducing ability) can have value beyond simply denying absorb: it can also reduce the opponent's granularity by forcing them to lose more defenders, and can deny soak on doomed units like Plexo Cell. Denying absorb is by far the most common use, but targeting granularity is a meaningful secondary application.
author: consensus
context: Multi-participant discussion about the full range of uses for the Thermite Core click ability.

insight: It is sometimes correct to soak incoming damage on a Wall rather than buy back two Engineers, because losing granularity against a mass-freeze or Thermite Core threat can be more punishing than the gold cost of the Wall.
author: supergreyman
context: Discussion of when to preserve granularity versus other defensive options against freeze armies.

insight: Vigilant attackers like Urban Sentry and Xeno Guardian are particularly strong against freeze strategies because they can block the freeze threat without losing their attack, unlike Steelsplitter which must be clicked to attack and therefore cannot both defend and deal damage in the same turn.
author: ruinedshadows
context: Discussion of how to use Urban Sentry and Xeno Guardian effectively, especially versus freeze.

insight: Massing Urban Sentry is generally mediocre because only the first one gets absorb value. However, soaking with them can be acceptable if the tempo gained is used to develop something powerful, such as massing Xeno Guardians to protect a Shadowfang strategy.
author: amalloy
context: Discussion of when and how to use Urban Sentry and Xeno Guardian in volume.

insight: Wall is a better early defensive buy than Steelsplitter because Wall is prompt and costs 1 less gold. Wall-then-Splitter (WS) costs the same total as Splitter-then-Wall (SW) if you want both, but WS blocks a damage immediately. Steelsplitter's non-promptness is a major drawback equivalent to losing roughly one Engineer.
author: amalloy
context: Discussion prompted by a newer player questioning why Steelsplitter is considered inferior to Wall for early defense.

insight: As a rule of thumb, a Blastforge opening should produce at most one Steelsplitter early; needing a second Steelsplitter usually indicates the Blastforge was bought too early. Buying Wall first and Steelsplitter second is preferred because it blocks damage immediately and leaves blue available for a more impactful unit next turn.
author: xyotsuba
context: Critique of advice given to a newer player about Steelsplitter sequencing in early blue openings.

insight: Clicking a Bombarder to kill a Wall is generally correct and analogous to clicking a Pixie to kill an Engineer; the exception is when the Bombarder can get absorb value by holding. A common mistake is using the dying (0-stamina) Bombarder to absorb when you should instead use it as soak, preserving the 1-stamina Bombarder's attack for killing the Wall.
author: amalloy
context: Tournament game review where clicking a 1-stamina Bombarder while absorbing on a 0-stamina one was identified as a misplay.

insight: "Granularity" is a concept largely novel to Prismata, referring to the number of small defensive units (especially Engineers) available to absorb or soak damage in fine increments. Losing granularity (e.g., having only a Wall rather than two Engineers) against abilities like Thermite Core can be severely punishing.
author: cactus_wren
context: Discussion about Prismata's unique terminology, with granularity cited as a concept not found in other card games.
